reviews.author: Sidney Plant wood
reviews.text: 150k to get in but so worth it. The best tourist attraction in Vietnam so far! It wasn’t too busy and we went just after lunch. As so we didn’t queue for ticket. When you walk up the stairs, to the first level ,you see the pavilion . This was an amazing building with great art work inside and out, around this pavilion there was statues of monks or warriors. The actual tomb was magnificent. I’ve never seen anything like it before in my life. The building itself was jaw dropping. Then you walk inside and you rub your eyes, because you can’t believe it. A building this old with this much detail and in pristine condition is incredible. Would definitely go back and would definitely recommend it.

reviews.author: Angelica Bosch
reviews.text: Located on Chau Chu Mountain near the city of Hue, Ung Mausoleum, also known as Khai Dinh Tomb, is one of the most interesting royal tombs in Vietnam. It was built for Emperor Khai Dinh, the 12th emperor of the Nguyen Dynasty. Construction began in 1920 and was completed in 1931. Unlike many other royal tombs in Hue, Khai Dinh Tomb combines traditional Vietnamese architecture with European influences. Visitors must climb a long staircase guarded by dragon sculptures to reach the main building. Inside the complex is Thien Dinh Palace, which contains the emperor’s bronze statue and his tomb beneath it. The interior is beautifully decorated with colorful mosaics made from broken porcelain and glass, as well as a famous dragon painting on the ceiling. Altogether a rather breathtaking sight. The entrance fee is about 150,000 VND (around €5–6), and the site is open daily from 7:00 AM to 5:30 PM.

reviews.author: Joyce Tan
reviews.text: Very beautiful design, rich in decorations, with French influence. Well maintained building external and internal very interesting and alot to see. High located on a hill with a great view. We'll worth it the visit and entrance fee.

reviews.author: Nguyen Le (John’s Tour Hue)
reviews.text: It is completely different from any other royal tomb in Vietnam. While most tombs are traditional and spread out in nature, Khai Dinh Tomb is a massive, grey concrete structure built into the side of a mountain. This tomb is a strange but amazing mix of Eastern and Western styles. You will see Gothic arches, Roman columns, and French influence blended with traditional Vietnamese dragons and motifs. The walls and ceilings are covered in incredibly detailed mosaics made from broken pieces of porcelain and glass. It is mind-blowing to think that artists hand-placed every single piece to create these colorful dragons and flowers. Above the King’s statue is a bronze canopy that looks like soft fabric but is actually made of tons of heavy concrete. Look up at the ceiling to see the "Nine Dragons Amidst Clouds" painting—legend says the artist painted it with his feet! The view from the top looking down at the rows of stone statues (Mandarin officials, horses, and elephants) is perfect for photos.

reviews.author: Anita L M
reviews.text: I am not easily impressed and this mausoleum utterly blew my mind. When we arrived, I stood at the bottom and immediately felt overwhelmed by how high up this landmark stood. I have bad arthritic knees and hips so I was already dreading the climb up so many flights of stairs. But we were here, and I was determined. We made our way up the first flight (also the flight with the most steps) and found ourselves on a landing to go through the turnstile and into the complex. Up another flight of stairs and we see rows of the Emperor's men guarding the space. After many photos, we made our way up yet another flight of stairs. Standing at the top was the massive building with architecture reflecting both Vietnamese and French designs. Dragons adorn the entrance, make sure you take your hats off when you enter the building and there is no flash photography allowed. The interior is just stunning. If you think the photos and videos are impressive, let me tell you, they do not do it justice at all. I was in awe of the sheer grandeur and ostentatious decor of them interior. Later, after reading more about Emperor Khai Dinh, it all made sense. This is a not-to-be-missed stop on your Hue itinerary. It is absolutely worth climbing you those steps for. Climbing down was frightening, but slowly and surely, we made it. I do not regret this whatsoever and it was one of the highlights of my trip to Hue.

reviews.author: Erik Blaney
reviews.text: Unbelievable piece of more recent history with over a Billion spent to make this masterpiece. Imported glass and iron from France create this stunning tomb dedicated to a not so nice emperor. I would suggest hiring a guide to tell you the history of its creation such as the ceiling being painted by a talented artist who painted it with his feet only to be later killed by the emperor so no one else would have the same ceiling. Stunning place

reviews.author: Brunch, Lunch & Munch with Sebastián Velázquez
reviews.text: In my opinion, the best tomb in Hue! The architecture is different from the rest and the details are so unique. Besides, according to our guide, this is the most beloved king by some Vietnamese.

reviews.author: Porntape Taperose
reviews.text: What an incredible visit to the Tomb of Emperor Khai Dinh! I traveled here with a group, and we were all truly impressed. The tomb itself is a masterpiece, blending Eastern and Western architecture in a way I've never seen before. The details are stunning—from the grand dragon staircase (as seen in our photos!) to the intricate mosaics inside. Beyond the amazing structure, the atmosphere is very special, and the views of the surrounding hills and nature are just beautiful. It was a memorable and wonderful experience for our entire group. Highly recommended for anyone visiting Vietnam!

reviews.author: LIMA LIMA
reviews.text: Located in Huế, this mausoleum was built between 1920 and 1931 for Khai Dinh, one of the last emperors of the Nguyễn dynasty. It is known for its blend of traditional Vietnamese and French architectural styles, as well as its richly decorated interior.

reviews.author: David Cristeal
reviews.text: Wonderful, visually interesting mausoleum and even better story of Khai Dinh. Second to last emperor of the Nguyen Dynasty and a puppet of the French government. Perhaps more popular due to Ho Chi Minh’s play about him, “the paper dragon”. And a nice not too long motor scooter ride from Hue.

reviews.author: Harry Lim
reviews.text: "A unique architectural masterpiece where East meets West" ​It is fascinating to learn that while Emperor Khai Dinh reigned for only 9 years, this tomb took 11 years to build—ultimately being completed by his son. ​The architecture is truly distinct from other imperial tombs in Hue. The way Western and Eastern aesthetics are cleverly blended together is impressive. The intricate glass and porcelain mosaics inside are breathtaking, and the fusion of styles creates a sophisticated atmosphere. It is definitely a must-visit spot to appreciate this unique harmony.

reviews.author: Rod and Linda Kodman
reviews.text: We had a wonderful guide that took us to this place. Be sure to cover your knees mid drift and shoulders. Try to make time to walk the grounds and see the beautiful statues spread around the property.

reviews.author: David Ribovski
reviews.text: Great place to visit. So much colture and history. I probably should have bought a ticket pack for all the sites to save some cash. Very inspirational. Great photo places

reviews.author: Alberto Fakes
reviews.text: This mausoleum is truly impressive. It is not only beautiful in its architecture but also rich in history, making it a fascinating place to explore. Surrounded by lush greenery, it offers a peaceful and serene atmosphere that enhances the overall experience. A perfect spot for anyone who enjoys culture, nature, and quiet moments of reflection.

reviews.author: Mr Ridan
reviews.text: This site is very impressive with detailed architecture that mixes Vietnamese and French styles. The mosaics inside are beautiful and worth seeing. However, it is quite crowded with many tourists, and the area is not very large, so the visit can feel a bit short compared to other tombs in Hue. I visited using a combo ticket that included several sites, which made it better value for money. Overall, it’s an interesting place to see, especially if you enjoy history and architecture, but don’t expect a quiet experience.

reviews.author: Mark LeVan
reviews.text: ​A truly fascinating site to explore. The fusion of French influence and traditional Vietnamese architecture is striking. It’s a bit bittersweet to see such grandeur knowing the Emperor raised land taxes by 30% to fund it—a move that was deeply unpopular at the time. It makes you wonder how modern Vietnamese citizens view these monuments today: as architectural treasures or symbols of excess

reviews.author: Rob
reviews.text: Absolutely stunning Mausoleum. Built over a span of 11 years this building is now been here over 100 years and no maintenance work has yet been done. The emperor is during 9 metres below the statue which was made as a gift by the French.

reviews.author: Erik Kerslake
reviews.text: Take a Grab bike to it. 50K Very beautiful up on the mountain. It's about a 30 minute visit max. To see it. Ticket 170k

reviews.author: Hunor Gocz
reviews.text: This place was simply stunning. We loved ot so much. The level of detail is just unreal. There are beautiful decorations in the inside. It is definitely worth visiting. There is a combined ticket if you want to visit more thombs along with the historical citadel so consider that to save money.

reviews.author: Zétény
reviews.text: Visiting Emperor Khai Dinh’s Mausoleum is an unforgettable experience. From the outside the dark never-ending stairs coupled with the figures signal mystery and the mightiness of the emperor. But upon entering the mausoleum something entirely different struck me thanks to the detailed and marvellous design: otherwordly beauty.

reviews.author: Ly Vuong
reviews.text: Beautiful and regal, but I was a bit disappointed for two reasons: 1. The ticket price is a bit high. I would expect VN government should sponsor, to encourage tourists to visit. 2. This place needs more upkeep, restoration, and explanations. Instead, lot of places were deteriorating, to the point illegible, washed out colors. Also, I would recommend to get the combo ticket.

reviews.author: Philip Blair
reviews.text: The mausoleum was pretty impressive with fantastically detailed decoration. A slightly over-long video with English subtitles gives useful background on this and other notable historic complexes in the area, so worth a watch if your eyes don't glaze over or your brain melts with the plethora of phonetically challenging characters. Seen on a very wet day.

reviews.author: S. Egberink
reviews.text: Impressive tomb, impressive story! Definitely worthwhile to climb the stairs; architecture, though partly decaying, as not seen before in Vietnam. Tip: read up on the tomb before you get there.

reviews.author: Delia Walsh
reviews.text: Really lovely palace. There aren't many information plaques so maybe the audioguide would be better for those wanting to know the history of the place. It's not very big and will only take 30-45 minutes (without an audioguide). There isn't too much shade so make sure to bring a hat on sunny days.

reviews.author: Alex Macfarlane
reviews.text: Nice to walk around. Inside the building is beautiful and well worth a visit. Also a beautiful view of the mountain. Just be warned there are 127 steps to the top! The statues were also very cool to see. Easy to get a taxi there and back from the centre of town. 150k entry per adult, worth getting the combo ticket at the citadel if you are visiting multiple tombs. Very busy at 3pm!

reviews.author: Perry Tee
reviews.text: 128 steps, very nice, toilets, did a tour so unsure if price. Worth a visit. Not very busy

reviews.author: John Winter
reviews.text: This was the first of the two tombs I visited—and it makes a striking impression. The climb up a long flight of stairs is definitely a bit of a workout, but the reward at the top is worth it. The exterior looks dark and commanding, and once inside, the level of detail in the decoration is stunning. The interiors are incredibly ornate, with intricate mosaics, vibrant tiles, and elaborate dragon motifs covering nearly every surface. It’s visually overwhelming in the best way—feels more like a palace shrine than a mausoleum. The contrast between the somber exterior and colorful interior really stood out. Definitely worth a visit if you’re in Huế. Tuesday 7 am–5:30 pm Wednesday. 7 am–5:30 pm Thursday 7 am–5:30 pm Friday 7 am–5:30 pm Saturday 7 am–5:30 pm Sunday 7 am–5:30 pm Monday 7 am–5:30 pm

reviews.author: Eddyy
reviews.text: Good for a 15 minutes short visit. Most likely you got to take taxi to get here. Get the combo ticket with Hue Citadel to get better deal. Beautiful architecture!

reviews.author: Artur Pytel
reviews.text: This is an absolute "must see", much better than The Citadel in the city center. The sarcophagus is gorgeous, you can enter the place wearing shorts.

reviews.author: Mark Espen
reviews.text: Nice place to visit. But will not take up too much of your time. Be prepared for a decent amount of stairs to climb.
